title: Lamé's theorem: Euclid's worst case is consecutive Fibonacci numbers (TAOCP Vol. 2 §4.5.3)
statement_informal: If the Euclidean algorithm performs n >= 1 division steps on a > b, then b >= F(n+1) and a >= F(n+2). Consecutive Fibonacci numbers are the smallest pair forcing a given number of steps — the tight O(log) bound behind the easy linear bound of euclid-gcd.

cost_model.notes: steps fuel a b counts the (a,b)->(b, a mod b) division steps (= snd (euclid ...) of euclid-gcd). The Fibonacci lower bound is proved by induction on fuel with the joint invariant fib(S n) <= b /\ fib(S(S n)) <= a.

references: Knuth, TAOCP Vol. 2 §4.5.3 — Lamé's theorem: the worst case of Algorithm E occurs at consecutive Fibonacci numbers, giving the tight O(log) step bound.
Builds on work-units/euclid-gcd (the easy linear bound snd(euclid)<=b) and the fibonacci-* corpus.
